title: Collaborative Evaluation Protocol for Creole Tomatoes in Participatory Breeding Programs
author: Almendra Cremaschi

steps.title: Define the Trial Objectives Participatory Tomato Breeding Co-design workshop
steps.instruction: Define the Trial Objectives Participatory Tomato Breeding Co-design workshop. Credits: Manuel Correa To ensure consistency, relevance, and comparability across trials and growing seasons, collaboratively define the purpose of the trial and the Target Product Profile before initiating the participatory evaluation process. The purpose of the trial may include varietal evaluation, participatory selection, and/or conservation of tomato accessions, and may form part of a broader participatory breeding process. The Target Product Profile consists of a structured description of the desired characteristics of a crop or variety, reflecting agronomic performance, culinary qualities, cultural relevance, and adaptation to local growing conditions. Together, these two elements guide the selection of appropriate seed materials and the prioritization of traits to be observed. Ensure that the selected traits align with the observation stages and fields included in the shared Observation Template provided in this protocol. If new traits are prioritized, the Observation Template should be adapted accordingly to preserve alignment across phenological stages and maintain comparability. The definition of the trial purpose and the TPP should be carried out through participatory processes, such as co-design workshops, surveys, or the collective analysis of results from previous evaluation cycles. These processes help ensure that trial objectives and evaluation criteria are aligned with participant priorities and contextual realities. An example of a co-design workshop is documented in the Bioleft participatory tomato workshop – FAUBA, 2020 .

steps.title: Set Up the On-Farm Trial Set...
steps.instruction: Set Up the On-Farm Trial Set up the trial under the usual cultivation conditions. There is no standardized plot size. Apply the same management practices across all accessions and avoid providing special treatment to any accession. The objective of the trial is to evaluate accession performance under real-life growing conditions, rather than under controlled or optimized experimental settings. Complete the following trial metadata fields: %s %s

steps.title: Define and Prepare Seed Materials Germination test. Credits
steps.instruction: Define and Prepare Seed Materials Germination test. Credits: Sabrina Rendón Once the trial objectives and the Target Product Profile are defined, select and prepare the tomato accessions for distribution. Source candidate accessions from public germplasm banks, previous participatory evaluation or breeding cycles, or seed-saving networks. Document provenance, multiplication history, and key metadata for each accession to support traceability throughout the trial. Select three (3) tomato accessions that are genetically distinct and representative of the traits prioritized for the current trial cycle. Ensure that the selection reflects both scientific criteria and participants’ preferences defined during the co-design phase. Perform germination tests when feasible to verify seed viability. A local check variety should be included as a reference. This can be either (i) a commonly grown commercial variety or (ii) a locally adapted farmer variety already known to the participant. The inclusion of a local check is optional and context-dependent, and aims to facilitate relative comparison under each participant’s conditions, rather than strict standardization across sites. Ensure sufficient seed quantity to provide each participant with at least 10 seeds per accession, securing a minimum of 6–8 viable plants per accession under typical growing conditions. The protocol does not require within-site replication (e.g., multiple plots per accession per participant). Instead, it follows a participatory and citizen science approach in which each participant represents a single observational unit. In this design, robustness is achieved through the aggregation of observations across a large number of participants and diverse environments, rather than through controlled replication within a single site. This allows the evaluation of varietal performance under real-world growing conditions while maintaining feasibility for non-expert participants (Van Etten et al., 2019). Assign each accession an unambiguous code or name (e.g., “Accession A,” “Accession B,” “Accession C”). Use this naming convention consistently across seed envelopes, the Observation Template, and all communication materials to prevent misidentification and data loss.

steps.title: Recruit and Onboard Participants Participant Selection...
steps.instruction: Recruit and Onboard Participants Participant Selection Criteria Participants include agroecological producers, family farmers, and gardeners with an interest in heirloom tomato varieties, whether for self-consumption or local markets under diverse, low-input conditions. Participation is particularly suited to those concerned with flavor, resilience, and biodiversity conservation. No formal training is required. Select participants based on their ability and willingness to implement the protocol under real growing conditions and to contribute actively to the participatory evaluation process. Apply the following criteria during participant selection: · Growing context: Prioritize agroecological producers, family farmers, and gardeners cultivating tomatoes under diverse, low-input conditions, including home gardens, community gardens, peri-urban plots, or small farms. · Commitment to participation: Select participants who demonstrate willingness to cultivate three tomato accessions, record observations throughout the growing cycle, and engage in peer learning and collective reflection activities. · Interest alignment: Give priority to participants with an interest in flavor, resilience, biodiversity conservation, and locally adapted tomato varieties, whether for self-consumption or local markets. · Geographic and contextual diversity: Seek a balanced distribution of participants across regions, agroecological conditions, and production contexts to strengthen comparative analysis and collective learning. · Continuity and learning: When possible, combine participants with prior experience in Bioleft campaigns and new participants, to support continuity while expanding the network. In participatory and citizen science-based trials, not all recruited participants complete the full observation cycle. In previous Bioleft campaigns, the effective participation rate—defined as participants who complete the observation process or provide sufficient data for analysis—has been approximately 15% (Cremaschi et al., 2025). For this reason, participant selection and onboarding prioritize commitment and continuity. Recent campaigns have worked with cohorts of approximately 30–40 participants , selected based on prior participation, demonstrated completion of previous trials, or explicit commitment to engage throughout the cycle. This approach seeks to balance feasibility with data quality and is consistent with distributed experimental designs, where robustness emerges from the aggregation of observations across participants and environments rather than from large initial recruitment alone (van Etten et al., 2020; Cremaschi et al., 2025). Participant Recruitment Process Recruit participants through an open and transparent call aligned with the objectives of the trial. · Disseminate the call through social media platforms (e.g. Instagram, Facebook, WhatsApp) and relevant networks related to agroecology, seed saving, and participatory breeding. · Invite interested individuals to complete a digital sign-up form to request seeds and join the trial. Use the form to collect basic personal information, growing context, and voluntary demographic data. A model sign-up form is available here Participatory Tomato Trial- Sign-Up Form.docx · Seek balanced participation in terms of gender, geographic distribution, and growing contexts. Support this objective through targeted outreach and inclusive form design. · To further support participation and data completeness, request selected participants to review and sign a participation commitment form prior to seed distribution. This document clarifies roles, expectations, and responsibilities throughout the evaluation process. A model of consent form is available here Informed Consent Form.docx

steps.title: Prepare and Distribute Materials Seed Packaging...
steps.instruction: Prepare and Distribute Materials Seed Packaging and Labeling of Tomato Accessions (Bioleft). Credits: Karina Schofield Prepare and distribute a complete package to each participant before the recommended planting period. Include the following information on each seed package and envelope: Accession name or code Place of origin Harvest year or seed multiplication date Complete distribution at least 2–3 weeks before the expected sowing date to allow time for soil preparation, planning, and clarification of questions. Distribute seed packages using one or more of the channels below. Track all deliveries to ensure receipt by participants. Use the seed distribution tracking database provided here Seed distribution tracking database.xlsx By mail: Send seed packages using registered post or courier services. Enable tracking and confirm reception with participants. By hand: Deliver seed packages in person during workshops, meetings, or through direct handover. Through local allies: Delegate distribution to trusted community-based organizations or local networks when appropriate. Provide each participant with a digital welcome kit to support implementation and communication throughout the trial. Include the following components: Observation Template (Excel-based): Send the Observation Template electronically (by email or WhatsApp). Ensure that fields are organized by phenological stage and accession. Provide basic guidance on how to complete the template, including where to enter observations and how to save and submit the file. Communication channel invitation: Share a digital invitation to the WhatsApp group or other designated communication channel used for ongoing support, coordination, and sharing of experiences throughout the campaign. Supplementary learning materials: Provide access to visual and written materials that support participants during the growing cycle, such as: Instructional videos (e.g., tutorials on tomato cultivation from seed to harvest, YouTube tutorial on how to grow tomatoes from seed to fruit ) Example cases, including reference photos and completed Observation Templates Ensure that all materials are accessible using commonly available devices and do not require specialized software beyond standard spreadsheet tools. Invite participants to a kick-off webinar or workshop to review the protocol, data sheet, and key observation stages. An example of a kick-off workshop is available here: https://youtu.be/fVAg0whFJ5w?si=_YuVxwdWlYxLZstF

steps.title: Initiate the Trial
steps.instruction: Initiate the Trial: Sowing and Early Growth Sowing. Credits: Patricio Devoto Sow seeds under conditions suitable for tomato germination. Select the sowing date according to local conditions. When possible, record environmental conditions at sowing, including: Day temperature: ideally between 21–27°C Night temperature: ideally between 13–18°C Relative humidity: ideally between 60–80% Maintain clear identification of each accession throughout all stages of the trial. To avoid mixing materials: Sow accessions separately If using seedbeds or nurseries, use separate containers for each accession Label containers, trays, and plants at every step Consider staggered sowing by planting half of the seeds initially and the remaining seeds 10–15 days later to reduce climatic risk and ensure the availability of healthy seedlings. If performing direct sowing in soil or containers where plants will remain until harvest, maintain accession identification with the same level of care. Record the following sowing and emergence observations for each evaluated accession in the provided spreadsheet, not directly in this protocol. %s %s

steps.title: Transplant to Containers or Soil Transplant...
steps.instruction: Transplant to Containers or Soil Transplant seedlings when they have at least two fully expanded leaves , moving them from the seedbed or nursery to their final growing location, either into containers or directly into the soil. Record the transplanting date in the Observation Template for each accession. Transplanting timing may be adjusted based on seedling size and local conditions, particularly to avoid exposure to frost or very low temperatures. Record the following transplanting information for each accession when seedlings are moved to containers or soil. If seedlings are transplanted to containers, complete the following information in the provided spreadsheet: %s %s Transplant to containers. Credits: Norma Quinteros If seedlings are transplanted to soil, complete the following information in the provided spreadsheet: %s %s Transplant to soil. Credits: Ailín Fernandez

steps.title: Crop Management Apply crop management practices...
steps.instruction: Crop Management Apply crop management practices according to your usual growing conditions throughout the tomato growing cycle. These practices may be carried out repeatedly and at different moments depending on plant development, environmental conditions, and management strategy. Record the date(s) and, where applicable, the frequency of each management practice in the Observation Template for each accession. Management practices are documented to contextualize varietal performance and to support interpretation of observed differences across accessions and environments. Pruning. Credits: Yael Tachdjian Record the following management practices for each accession in the provided spreadsheet, indicating the date(s) and, when applicable, the number of times each practice was carried out. %s %s

steps.title: Growth and Development Observe plant growth...
steps.instruction: Growth and Development Observe plant growth and development following transplanting, with particular attention to phenological progression, stress responses, plant health, and uniformity. Observations during this phase capture how each accession responds to local growing conditions and management practices over time. Record the specified growth and development observations in the Observation Template for each accession. Ratings and dates are based on visual assessment and comparative judgment and are intended to support interpretation of varietal performance rather than formal diagnosis. Record the following growth and development information for each accession in the provided spreadsheet. %s %s * Plant death due to pests and diseases When relevant to the local context, facilitators may include specific priority diseases (e.g., Tomato brown rugose fruit virus, bacterial wilt, or other regionally prevalent pathogens) as explicit variables in the observation template. The selection of target diseases should be adapted to the most significant phytosanitary constraints of each region. In this case, the protocol should be accompanied by context-specific identification tools designed to support participants with different levels of technical knowledge and literacy. For example, visual identification guides combining photographs and simple descriptions of symptoms—such as the Bioleft ToBRFV guide (see pages 1–2 for symptom descriptions and pages 3–7 for visual references)—can support more reliable field observations and reporting. When disease identification is uncertain, participants are encouraged to describe symptoms rather than assign a formal diagnosis. This approach prioritizes observational consistency while remaining accessible to non-expert users. Bioleft - VIRUS RUGOSO DEL TOMATE.pdf

steps.title: Practices During Fruiting Observe and document...
steps.instruction: Practices During Fruiting Observe and document any management practices applied during the fruiting period, from early fruit set until the onset of harvest. These practices are optional and context-dependent and may be applied to manage plant vigor, fruit development, or maturation under local growing conditions. Record the date(s) and a brief description of each practice in the Observation Template for each accession. This information supports interpretation of fruit size, uniformity, maturation timing, and harvest outcomes, and does not imply that such practices are required. Record the following management practices during fruiting for each accession in the provided spreadsheet. %s %s

steps.title: Harvest and Post-harvest Harvest fruits when...
steps.instruction: Harvest and Post-harvest Harvest fruits when they reach eating maturity according to local use and preference, which in most cases corresponds to near-full coloration. Observations in this phase focus on harvest characteristics, yield, and short-term post-harvest performance under household storage conditions. Record the following harvest and post-harvest information in the Observation Template for each accession. Measurements and estimates are intended to be practical and feasible under on-farm or household conditions. %s %s * Post-harvest duration (days) When relevant, participants may also record post-harvest duration under refrigerated conditions. In such cases, the storage condition should be clearly specified in the Observation Template. If both measurements are available, participants are encouraged to report both values to support comparative analysis.

steps.title: At the end of the evaluation...
steps.instruction: At the end of the evaluation cycle, all Observation Templates completed by participants are collected by the Bioleft facilitation team and consolidated into a centralized database. This process involves cleaning, harmonizing, and validating the data to ensure consistency across participants, accessions, and observation stages. Consolidation is carried out using the automatically generated fields from the final sheet of each Observation Template, which enables aggregation while preserving traceability to individual trials and contexts. Based on the consolidated database, the facilitation team produces a collective results report that synthesizes agronomic, sensory, and experiential findings from the campaign. The report presents comparative analyses across accessions, highlights patterns observed under different growing conditions, and documents participant interpretations and reflections. An example of such a synthesis is provided in the report “Mejoramiento Participativo de Tomate: Aprendizajes de la campaña 2024–2025” (attached), which illustrates how distributed observations are transformed into shared knowledge outputs DT4-CENIT-Bioleft-Mejoramiento-Participativo-de-Tomate.-Aprendizajes-de-la-campana-de-2024-2025.pdf . To close the cycle, Bioleft organizes a collective feedback workshop in which results are presented and discussed with participants, breeders, researchers, and partner organizations. These workshops create a space for collective interpretation of the findings, reflection on methodological aspects of the trial, and discussion of implications for future selection, conservation, or breeding cycles. A recorded example of a feedback workshop is available here: https://youtu.be/Xhrdqwst1u0?si=nqDTMGlzCWn5G2OF Insights emerging from data consolidation, reports, and collective discussions may inform revisions to the protocol, improvements to observation tools, and the definition of priorities for subsequent campaigns. In this way, data consolidation and feedback function not only as an analytical step, but as a core component of the participatory learning and co-design process.

steps.title: Sensory Evaluation Participatory tasting session for...
steps.instruction: Sensory Evaluation Participatory tasting session for sensory evaluation of creole tomato accessions. Credits: Sofía Casals. The protocol includes two complementary instances of sensory evaluation: 1. Participant-based evaluation (on-farm)All participants (producers, gardeners) evaluate the sensory qualities of the tomatoes they cultivate as part of the observation template. This includes attributes such as flavor, texture, aroma, and overall appreciation. These evaluations are conducted under real consumption conditions and reflect situated, experience-based knowledge. 2. Consumer-based evaluation (collective tasting events)When feasible, sensory evaluation is complemented by collective tasting events involving external consumers. These may include local community members, market actors, or institutional participants (e.g., students, researchers, chefs). The objective is to capture a broader range of preferences beyond producers’ perspectives. Consumers are typically recruited through open invitations, local networks, or institutional partnerships, depending on the context of implementation. Number of evaluators The protocol does not impose a strict minimum number of evaluators, as implementation contexts may vary. However: Participant-based evaluation includes all trial participants. For consumer-based evaluations, a minimum of 20–30 evaluators is recommended when feasible to support more robust interpretation of preferences. In smaller or informal contexts, lower numbers may still provide valuable qualitative insights, particularly when combined with participant evaluations. Both sources of information are considered complementary and are integrated in the collective analysis phase to support selection decisions. This protocol promotes a community-based and educational sensory evaluation, in which creole (heirloom) tomatoes are tasted and evaluated based on perceived flavor, aroma, texture, and appearance. Sensory evaluation is intended to capture participant preferences and experiential knowledge rather than expert or laboratory-based measurements. Evaluations are conducted using a 9-point hedonic scale, where: 1 = dislike very much 5 = neither like nor dislike (neutral) 9 = like very much Participants complete the sensory evaluation after harvest, either individually or collectively, depending on local context. %s %s
